1_answer_correctness.justification: Notebook code: eval imported from dataset pkg (benchmark/infrastructure.py run_agent_loop) scores status['online']/status['total'] from a real tool-using agent loop with deterministic file/run operations; pass/fail keys are computed from actual environment state, not authored answer strings.

2_construct_validity.justification: Task page (markdown prose) claims an adaptive infrastructure-building agentic benchmark; notebook code (traced into tasks.py adaptive_learning + infrastructure.py run_agent_loop) genuinely drives the model through write_file/read_file/update_file/run/give_up tool calls and scores the resulting environment, so claim and code align and the model is truly measured.

3_code_quality.justification: Notebook code: well-structured package (tasks.py, infrastructure.py, task_generator.py, prompts/system.md) with clear agent loop, separation of generators T1..T5, and readable helpers; the notebook AttributeError on LocalEnvironment is an environmental version mismatch, not a logic defect.

4_sample_size.justification: Notebook code: TaskSpecGenerator tasks_count=50 across 5 increasing-complexity tiers gives ample independent items for a stable agentic score.

5_metric_design.justification: Notebook code: graded continuous score = online services / total required services from real environment inspection, rewarding partial progress rather than a brittle all-or-nothing match.

6_problem_statement.justification: Task-page prose: the markdown/docstring sets up the adaptive infrastructure-building premise but is thin and setup-oriented, lacking a fully developed motivation/spec of what good performance means.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Task page + notebook: prompts/system.md and terminal .md docs plus inline structure thoroughly document the agent interface, tools, and scoring.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Task page + notebook: the agentic tool-calling loop with escalating tiers cleanly targets multi-step infrastructure/agent capability rather than a narrow trivia skill.

9_item_diversity.justification: Notebook code: 10 distinct generators across 5 complexity tiers produce structurally varied tasks, avoiding near-duplicate items.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Notebook code: deterministic word-count and forbidden-token lexical checks are sound, but pass/fail ultimately rests on a subjective 4-judge LLM panel at a 75% threshold, so correctness keys are partly opinion-based rather than verifiable.

2_construct_validity.justification: Task page claims to measure metacognition/empathy; notebook code (llm.prompt + lexical gating + judge panel) actually measures constrained creative writing and instruction-following, a real model eval but with a substantial construct gap/confound.

3_code_quality.justification: Notebook code: functional but heavily copy-pasted across 5 near-identical notebooks with duplicated logic and little abstraction.

4_sample_size.justification: Notebook code: each notebook is a single @kbench.task with one prompt -> 5 items total, far too few for a stable score.

5_metric_design.justification: Notebook code: combines deterministic lexical checks with a multi-judge rubric, but the 75% judge threshold makes the headline metric noisy and partly subjective.

6_problem_statement.justification: Task-page prose: only a one-line description plus docstring; no developed problem statement explaining the construct, motivation, or success criteria.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Task page + notebook: minimal inline comments and a thin description document the flow only partially; judge criteria are present but not well explained.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Task page + notebook: aims at empathy/metacognition but the implementation targets lexical constraint compliance, so capability targeting is loose.

9_item_diversity.justification: Notebook code: 5 different historical-figure prompts give some variety but all share one identical template/format.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Notebook code: ground-truth answers are generated programmatically for World-Swap problems and checked deterministically against structured llm.prompt(schema=UnifiedResp) output, so correctness keys are verifiable.

2_construct_validity.justification: Task page claims adversarial metacognition profiling; notebook code computes SDT metrics (d', meta-d', M-ratio), ECE, asymmetric Brier (ACE), and selective prediction over real model answers with a scaffold-equalized Tier1 to remove confounds, tightly matching the claim.

3_code_quality.justification: Notebook code: clean, modular implementation of generators, metrics, bootstrap CIs (5000 resamples), and slop detection with clear structure.

4_sample_size.justification: Notebook code: N=600 problems across 4 tiers x 4 types provides a large, well-stratified sample.

5_metric_design.justification: Notebook code: sophisticated multi-metric design (meta-d'/M-ratio, ECE, ACE, selective prediction scoring +1/0/-5, tool utility) with bootstrap 95% CIs and per-tier breakdown.

6_problem_statement.justification: Task-page prose: strong markdown sections describe the metacognition construct, but the public-facing statement is somewhat technical/dense rather than a fully self-contained motivating problem statement.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Task page + notebook: extensive markdown plus inline documentation explain metrics, generators, and confound controls thoroughly.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Task page + notebook: directly targets metacognitive calibration/uncertainty via SDT and selective prediction, a precise capability focus.

9_item_diversity.justification: Notebook code: 4 problem types (operator_swap, physics, linguistic, logic) across 4 difficulty tiers yield high structural diversity.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Notebook code: the model-calling eval (temporalbench-eval) uses brittle substring matching and staleness-detection uses keyword matching on mock items, so correctness keys are weak; the headline thesis tasks assert predicted is not None on the authors' own store, which is not model correctness.

2_construct_validity.justification: Task page claims 'validity windows beat decay' temporal benchmark; notebook code for the central thesis tasks (temporalbench-v2-pccc / v4: PastQueryTrap, ChangeDetection, etc.) takes llm but NEVER calls it, instead asserting against the authors' own TemporalAttentionStoreWithValidity and scoring 942/942, 734/734 by construction -- the headline eval does not measure a model.

3_code_quality.justification: Notebook code: spread across 5 inconsistent notebooks with mock hardcoded items, by-construction assertions, and brittle matching, indicating low overall quality.

4_sample_size.justification: Notebook code: the real model-facing tasks operate over large item sets (e.g. 2000-item CSV for temporal-ambiguity, 734/942-row stores), so raw item counts are ample where the model is actually queried.

5_metric_design.justification: Notebook code: headline metrics are by-construction pass counts (942/942) disconnected from model output, and the model-calling variants rely on fragile substring/keyword matching, so the metric does not validly measure model performance.

6_problem_statement.justification: Task-page prose: the markdown articulates the 'validity windows beat decay' thesis but reads as an argument for the authors' store design rather than a clean problem statement for the model under test.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Task page + notebook: some inline explanation exists but it is inconsistent across the 5 notebooks and conflates the authors' store with the model eval.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Task page + notebook: temporal reasoning is the intended capability, but because the central tasks never query the model, targeting of model capability is only partial (limited to the auxiliary model-calling notebooks).

9_item_diversity.justification: Notebook code: several query types exist (PastQueryTrap, ChangeDetection, OverlapQuery, etc.) but many share a single store template and some rely on a few mock items, limiting genuine diversity.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Notebook code: solutions are validated deterministically -- code-golf executes submissions against hidden tests, regex-golf uses re.fullmatch against MATCH/DO_NOT_MATCH lists with pre-validated baselines, so pass/fail is objectively verifiable.

2_construct_validity.justification: Task page claims a constraint-adaptation benchmark; notebook code genuinely calls the model with iterative feedback (llm.prompt over 10 trials) and scores fractional length reduction / geometric movement on real outputs, closely matching the claim that it measures adapting to constraints.

3_code_quality.justification: Notebook code: clean evaluators with robust error handling (syntax/logic guards, system-role-rejection fallback) and clear feedback construction across all three notebooks.

4_sample_size.justification: Notebook code: 15 items total (5 code-golf + 5 regex sessions + 5 EGAT prompts) -- adequate but modest, partly offset by 10-trial reprompting per item.

5_metric_design.justification: Notebook code: fractional character/length reduction is a sensible graded metric, but the -1.0 penalty per failed session and small session count make the aggregate sensitive to single failures.

6_problem_statement.justification: Task-page prose: clear per-notebook descriptions (e.g. Regex Golf scoring table and session overview) but no unified, fully motivated benchmark-level problem statement.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Task page + notebook: thorough markdown (scoring formulas, session tables) plus well-commented code document the eval end to end.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Task page + notebook: compression-under-constraints (regex/code golf, embedding geometry) precisely targets constraint-adaptation/optimization capability.

9_item_diversity.justification: Notebook code: three distinct task families (code golf, regex golf, EGAT embedding geometry) with varied sub-tasks give strong structural diversity.
